# UC-10.6.1 · Critical Vulnerability Trending

## Description

Tracking critical vulnerabilities over time measures security posture improvement and identifies remediation stalls.

## Value

Tracking critical vulnerabilities over time measures security posture improvement and identifies remediation stalls.

## Implementation

Ingest scan results from vulnerability management platform. Track unique vulnerabilities by severity over time. Alert when critical count exceeds threshold or increases. Report on remediation progress weekly.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Tenable Add-On for Splunk` (Splunkbase 4060), `Qualys Technology Add-on (TA) for Splunk` (Splunkbase 2964).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Vulnerability scan results (severity, CVE, affected asset).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest scan results from vulnerability management platform. Track unique vulnerabilities by severity over time. Alert when critical count exceeds threshold or increases. Report on remediation progress weekly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vulnerability sourcetype="tenable:vuln"
| where severity IN ("Critical","High")
| timechart span=1d dc(cve_id) as unique_vulns by severity
```

Understanding this SPL

**Critical Vulnerability Trending** — Tracking critical vulnerabilities over time measures security posture improvement and identifies remediation stalls.

Documented **Data sources**: Vulnerability scan results (severity, CVE, affected asset). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Tenable Add-On for Splunk` (Splunkbase 4060), `Qualys Technology Add-on (TA) for Splunk` (Splunkbase 2964). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vulnerability; **sourcetype**: tenable:vuln. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=vulnerability, sourcetype="tenable:vuln". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where severity IN ("Critical","High")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1d** buckets with a separate series **by severity** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities where Vulnerabilities.severity IN ("critical","high") by Vulnerabilities.cve,Vulnerabilities.dest
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Critical Vulnerability Trending** — Tracking critical vulnerabilities over time measures security posture improvement and identifies remediation stalls.

Documented **Data sources**: Vulnerability scan results (severity, CVE, affected asset). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Tenable Add-On for Splunk` (Splunkbase 4060), `Qualys Technology Add-on (TA) for Splunk` (Splunkbase 2964). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Groups by CVE and destination so you can compare counts to your index search and vendor console.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare severities, CVEs, and affected hosts in Splunk with the same time range in Tenable.io and in Qualys VMDR or Cloud Agent UI. For a few sampled CVEs, compare details with NIST NVD. Verify field extractions, lookups, and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (vuln count trend by severity), Single value (critical vuln count), Bar chart (top CVEs), Table (critical vulnerabilities).

## SPL

```spl
index=vulnerability sourcetype="tenable:vuln"
| where severity IN ("Critical","High")
| timechart span=1d dc(cve_id) as unique_vulns by severity
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities where Vulnerabilities.severity IN ("critical","high") by Vulnerabilities.cve,Vulnerabilities.dest
```

## Visualization

Line chart (vuln count trend by severity), Single value (critical vuln count), Bar chart (top CVEs), Table (critical vulnerabilities).
